dialogue.utterance: Dispatch for Unit 7 to 7/11 on W. Grace Street for breathing problem.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT. What made you call 911 today?
I've had shortness of breath for about an hour now.
You appear alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Let me check using the AVPU scale.
Your eyes are open spontaneously, and you're aware of your surroundings.
You're answering my questions clearly and in a logical manner.
You're moving both your arms and legs independently - great motor response.
Your airway is patent, with no signs of obstruction.
Breathing is rapid at 30 per minute. I notice wheezing, especially on expiration.
Pulse is strong and regular. Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color, no cyanosis.
Your pulse is 111, respiratory rate is 30, and your heart rhythm is sinus.
Blood pressure is very elevated - 246 over 125.
Oxygen saturation is 86, which is below normal. We'll treat that now.
Blood glucose is 162, which is within a safe range for now.
Your skin remains dry, warm, and appears normal - no signs of mottling or cyanosis.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light - PERRL.
Bilateral expiratory wheezes are still audible. This may indicate airway constriction.
I've initiated four-lead EKG monitoring. Your heart rhythm is sinus with occasional premature complexes.
I will now administer a nebulizer treatment for better bronchodilation.
It's a sinus rhythm with occasional supraventricular extras but very stable otherwise.
I've started an 18-gauge IV in your left antecubital vein for medication administration.
I will combine albuterol and ipratropium to support your airway.
Your pulse has decreased to 100, and your respiratory rate is 20 now.
Blood pressure is now 208 over 104 - still elevated but showing early stabilization.
Oxygen saturation has improved to 96%, which is a welcome response to treatment.
Unable to obtain a reliable reading from the glucometer.
Have you experienced pain or discomfort since the shortness of breath started?
No pain at all - I'm just having trouble catching my breath.
What was happening when the shortness of breath first started?
I was in the store when I walked into a can, lost my balance, and fell on the floor.
When did you last have something to eat or drink?
I had juice and toast earlier this morning before the episode.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take something for blood pressure and kidney disease, but I don't know the names right now.
Any allergies I need to be aware of?
I'm allergic to penicillin.
I'll note zero pain in your record.
I see you mention you have hypertension and stage 4 kidney disease. Is that correct?
Yes. I have high blood pressure and require dialysis, but I haven't had it recently.
Have you had any chest pain, nausea, or vomiting?
No chest pain or vomiting-just the rapid breathing and initial lightheadedness.
Did you feel dizziness at all?
I felt a little lightheaded at the start but am more stable now.
Your neck veins are not distended. No rigidity or swelling noted.
Head is intact, no signs of trauma, lacerations, or swelling.
No bleeding or discharge from ears, nose, or mouth is present.
Abdomen is soft, no pain or distention noted.
Chest is stable, no deformities, no displacement.
No abnormalities are present on your back. Spine is stable.
Pelvis appears intact and not displaced.
No signs of airway blockage visible.
The fall wasn't witnessed, but an employee eventually helped me up.
Eyes clear, no conjunctival injection or trauma noted.
You do not have a history of COPD or asthma, but you do smoke. Do you use an inhaler?
I don't have an inhaler. I smoke but have never required one.
Bowel sounds are present and no discomfort is elicited.
Wheezing is still audible in both upper and lower lobes, same as before treatment.
You had your last food and fluids earlier in the day and none since.
Skin still looks normal-unexpected to show mottling given the BP.
I will now position you on the stretcher for safe removal. You'll be placed in a 5-point harness.
I have now placed a 12-lead EKG. It shows sinus tachycardia with possible left ventricular hypertrophy.
Based on assessment and treatment, we will transport you directly to VCU Emergency for full evaluation.
I am following the respiratory distress protocol for conditions like exacerbations.
You're secured with a 5-point harness and receiving continuous oxygen.
The second EKG reconfirms sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs and no acute myocardial infarction indicators.
Breathing is at 18 per minute. You're doing well; wheezing is still present.
Your pulse is now 88, blood pressure 162/90, and oxygen saturation at 100%.
I will continue to monitor breathing and vitals during the transport process.
I'll use a two-person lift to transfer you from the stretcher to the hospital bed.
Primary and secondary protocols are completed. We are now ready for transport.
Vital signs are stable at pulse 88, BP 162/90, Spo2 100%, and respiratory rate 18.
You continue to show wheezing despite treatment, but overall stability is maintained.
Still no pain reported during my latest reassessment.
We'll arrive to VCU ER shortly, where I will reassess you one last time.
No change in your condition-still no pain or new symptoms.
I performed a stroke scale evaluation which was negative.
No new procedures were performed after IV placement and nebulizer therapy.
Lung sounds were rechecked on arrival - bilateral wheezing continued.
No additional interventions were required.
Patient was managed using standard protocol for temporary airway issues.
Full report given to staff RN. All equipment is ready for turnover.
The EKG remains in sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST and T wave abnormalities.
Patient's extremities have intact sensory and motor function with normal pulses bilaterally.
Vital signs improved over time with controlled care and non-emergent transport.
Patient condition remains stable for the time of handover at VCU.
